summary: Japan's approach is evaluation-led. CRYPTREC, the national cryptographic evaluation body, publishes post-quantum cryptography guidelines and research surveys, and a cross-ministerial council under the Cabinet Secretariat promotes the use of post-quantum cryptography across government agencies. The Digital Agency carries the migration among its trust-policy priorities.
govActors:
CRYPTREC | National cryptographic technology evaluation body, publishes PQC guidelines
Cabinet Secretariat | Runs the cross-ministerial council on government PQC adoption
Digital Agency | Carries PQC migration in its trust-policy priorities
provenance: CRYPTREC guidelines + FY2024 survey: ingested CRYPTREC rows
Digital Agency priorities: FY2026 budget row
Mixed process: CRYPTREC national evaluation framework processing NIST standards - REVIEW
No timeline/target set: 2035 figure appears only in vendor sources, excluded per iron rule
Model 2026-06-14 (NIST-bloc / contextualiser, Medium): CRYPTREC evaluates and lists the NIST algorithms; no national algorithm. Reclassified from Mixed.
classificationBasis:
CRYPTREC guidelines + FY2024 survey: ingested CRYPTREC rows
FIPS 203/204/205: CRYPTREC evaluation-committee report (ingested)
Cross-ministerial council: Cabinet Secretariat row
Digital Agency priorities: FY2026 budget row
Mixed process: CRYPTREC national evaluation framework processing NIST standards - REVIEW
No timeline/target set: 2035 figure appears only in vendor sources, excluded per iron rule
mainRegulation:
CRYPTREC Ciphers List for e-Government procurement (LS-0001-2022R2) | national | binding-by-market-access
Common Standards for Cybersecurity Measures for Government Agencies | national | binding-by-market-access
CRYPTREC Cryptographic Technology Guideline on Post-Quantum Cryptography (FY2024 edition) | national | guidance
Liaison Council on the Use of Post-Quantum Cryptography in Government Agencies | national | soft-law
obligation: Government agencies procuring information systems must, under the Common Standards set by the Cybersecurity Strategic Headquarters, specify ciphers from the CRYPTREC Ciphers List in their procurement requirements, which makes the list binding by market access for government suppliers. This currently governs cryptography generally rather than mandating post-quantum algorithms; CRYPTREC has evaluated and is adding the NIST post-quantum algorithms (ML-KEM and others), and its post-quantum guideline remains advisory. There is no general post-quantum obligation on the private sector.
